title: Blood in Cat Urine: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticing blood in your cat's urine? Learn about hematuria, urgency levels, and why documenting the symptoms helps your veterinarian triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood in a cat's urine (hematuria) requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours, but if your cat is straining and unable to pass any urine, it is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate care.

What does it mean if there is blood in my cat’s urine?

Finding drops of blood in your cat's urine, a condition known as hematuria, indicates that there is inflammation or injury somewhere in the urinary tract. This can occur in the kidneys, the bladder, or the urethra. In cats, this is frequently associated with Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD), which can be caused by:

  • Urinary tract infections
  • Bladder stones
  • Stress-induced inflammation

Is blood in my cat's urine a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for blood in the urine is Medium.
  • It is generally not an immediate life-threat if your cat is still behaving normally and successfully passing urine.
  • You should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Critical: If your cat is straining and unable to produce any urine, this is a life-threatening emergency known as a urinary blockage; you must seek help immediately.

Why should I take a photo of the urine to help with triage?

  • If you can safely do so, a clear photograph of the urine drops is a vital tool for your veterinarian.
  • The specific shade of the blood—ranging from light pink to dark tea-colored—helps the vet determine the severity.
  • Identifying visible clots or mucus helps the vet find the potential source of the bleeding before they even begin the physical examination.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, hematuria (blood in urine) can be a clinical sign of feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD), with potential underlying causes including urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis (urinary stones), and sterile cystitis (feline interstitial cystitis). Urolithiasis, with common stone types being calcium oxalate, magnesium ammonium phosphate, and urate, is frequently suspected based on hematuria, dysuria, or urethral obstruction. Diagnostic evaluation should include urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography to differentiate uroliths from other conditions. Radiography, cystoscopy, or ultrasonography are important for urolith detection, especially for smaller stones that may require double-contrast radiography. Increased white blood cells, red blood cells, and/or bacteria on urinalysis are consistent with cystitis, but a urine culture and antimicrobial susceptibility test are recommended if infection is suspected, ideally performed on a sample collected via cystocentesis or sterile urethral catheterization and processed promptly.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Can stress cause blood in a cat's urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress can lead to Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC), a condition where the bladder wall becomes inflamed and bleeds without a bacterial infection being present.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is also crying while using the litter box?
faqs.answer: Vocalizing while urinating suggests pain. While blood alone is a medium urgency, signs of significant pain should prompt a more immediate call to your local clinic or an emergency vet.

faqs.question: Will blood in the urine go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While some inflammatory episodes may seem to resolve, the underlying cause usually requires medical treatment or dietary changes to prevent recurrence or worsening.
